title: US Visa Photo Requirements (2026 Guide) – Size, Background, Dress Code & Official Rules
description: Complete US visa photo requirements guide 2026. Learn official size (600x600), background rules, dress code, glasses policy, and how to avoid rejection.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
US Visa Photo Requirements (2026 Official Guide)

If you're applying for a US visa from the US, UK, or Europe, your photo is one of the most important parts of your application. Even a small mistake—such as incorrect size, shadows, or wearing glasses—can lead to rejection or delays.

The US Department of State has strict photo requirements, and they are enforced automatically during DS-160 submission. This guide explains everything in a clear and practical way so you can avoid rejection.

What is the Correct US Visa Photo Size (600x600 Explained)

The official US visa photo size for digital submission is 600 x 600 pixels. The image must be square, meaning equal width and height.

  • Minimum: 600 x 600 pixels
  • Maximum: 1200 x 1200 pixels
  • Format: JPEG (.jpg)
  • Max file size: 240 KB

Your face must occupy 50% to 69% of the image height. The eyes should be positioned between 56% and 69% from the bottom.

Incorrect sizing is one of the most common reasons for DS-160 photo rejection.

US Visa Photo Background Requirements (White or Off-White)

The background must be plain white or off-white. It should be clean, evenly lit, and completely free from shadows or objects.

Avoid these mistakes:

  • Shadows behind your head
  • Textured or colored walls
  • Uneven lighting

The system uses biometric detection, so a clean background is essential.

Face Position and Expression Rules

Your face must be directly facing the camera with a neutral expression.

  • Eyes open and clearly visible
  • Mouth closed (no smiling)
  • No head tilt
  • Full face visible from chin to top of head

The image should be centered both horizontally and vertically.

US Visa Photo Dress Code: What to Wear

There is no strict dress code, but guidelines must be followed.

  • Wear regular everyday clothing
  • Choose darker colors for contrast
  • Avoid white or light-colored outfits

Not allowed:

  • Uniforms (except religious)
  • Hats or caps
  • Heavy accessories

Head coverings are allowed only for religious or medical reasons, but your full face must remain visible.

Can You Take a US Visa Photo at Home?

Yes, you can take a US visa photo at home if you follow the official guidelines carefully.

Step-by-step process:

  1. Stand against a plain white wall
  2. Use natural or soft lighting
  3. Keep camera at eye level
  4. Maintain neutral expression
  5. Crop image to 600x600 pixels

Avoid filters or editing that changes your appearance. The photo must be clear, sharp, and recent (within 6 months).

US Visa Photo Rules for Infants & Babies

For infants and babies, the rules are slightly relaxed but still important.

  • No other person should be visible
  • Baby must face the camera
  • Eyes should be open if possible

Tip: Lay the baby on a white sheet and take the photo from above.

US Visa Photo Rules for Glasses & Head Coverings

Glasses are not allowed in US visa photos. Even clear lenses can cause glare or reflections.

Head coverings are allowed only if:

  • They are worn for religious or medical reasons
  • Your face is fully visible
  • No shadows are cast on your face

Common Mistakes That Cause Rejection

  • Wrong size (not 600x600)
  • Shadows on face or background
  • Blurry or low-quality image
  • Incorrect face positioning
  • Wearing glasses

How to Create a US Visa Photo Online

Manually editing your photo can be difficult and error-prone. Instead, you can use an AI tool to automatically generate a compliant visa photo.

These tools handle:

  • Background removal
  • Face alignment
  • Correct sizing (600x600)

👉 Try it here: Create US Visa Photo Online

Conclusion

US visa photo requirements are strict, but easy to follow if you understand the rules. By ensuring correct size, background, and lighting, you can avoid delays and submit your application with confidence.

Taking a few minutes to get your photo right can save days or even weeks in processing time.

title: Schengen Visa Photo Requirements 2026 – Size, Specs & Free Online Maker Tool
description: Complete Schengen visa photo guide 2026. Learn the correct size (35x45mm), background rules, face specs, and use our free online Schengen visa photo maker tool to get a compliant photo in minutes.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
Schengen Visa Photo: The Complete 2026 Requirements Guide

Your Schengen visa photo is the first thing a consulate officer reviews. Get it wrong — wrong size, shadows, or a colored background — and your entire application can be delayed or rejected before it's even assessed. The rules are strict, standardized across all 27 Schengen member states, and enforced using biometric software that catches errors automatically.

This guide covers every official Schengen visa photo specification for 2026: correct dimensions, background color, face position, dress code, and the best way to use a free Schengen visa photo maker tool to generate a compliant image in under two minutes — without leaving your home.

Who Needs a Schengen Visa?

The Schengen Area covers 27 European countries — including France, Germany, Spain, Italy, the Netherlands, and Switzerland — that share open internal borders. A single Schengen visa allows you to travel freely within this entire zone for up to 90 days in any 180-day period.

Citizens of many countries, including India, China, Nigeria, Pakistan, and most of Southeast Asia, must apply for a Schengen visa before traveling. Nationals of the US, UK, Canada, and Australia are currently exempt for short tourist stays, but may still need a visa for long-stay or work purposes. Regardless of your nationality, if you need a Schengen visa photo, the specifications below apply to you.

Schengen Visa Photo Size: The Official Specifications

The Schengen visa photo size follows the ICAO (International Civil Aviation Organization) biometric standard, adopted uniformly across all member state consulates.

  • Dimensions: 35 mm wide × 45 mm tall
  • Schengen visa photo size in cm: 3.5 cm × 4.5 cm
  • Digital equivalent: 413 × 531 pixels at 300 DPI
  • Face height: 32–36 mm (chin to crown of head)
  • Face coverage: 70–80% of total photo height
  • Format: Color JPEG for digital; matte or semi-gloss print for physical submission
  • Recency: Taken within the last 6 months

This is a rectangular portrait format — taller than it is wide — unlike the square US visa photo. Submitting a cropped square image, even at the correct face ratio, will result in an immediate rejection. Always confirm the exact digital pixel dimensions required by the specific embassy portal you are submitting to, as some VFS Global and BLS submission systems may specify slightly different pixel values while maintaining the same 35×45mm aspect ratio.

Background Requirements: White Only

The background in your Schengen visa photo must be plain white or very light grey. This is one of the most frequently failed requirements — and one of the easiest to fix with the right tool.

  • Solid white or near-white — no cream, beige, blue, or pastel tones
  • No shadows cast from your head or shoulders
  • No objects, walls, furniture, or patterns in the frame
  • Evenly lit — no bright hotspots or dark corners

If you're shooting a DIY Schengen visa photo at home, stand at least 1 metre away from the wall to prevent shadow. Diffused natural light from a window directly in front of you works well. Avoid harsh overhead or side lighting. Alternatively, a free Schengen visa photo editor with AI background removal can instantly replace any background with the correct white — making DIY much more reliable.

Face Position, Expression, and Lighting Rules

Biometric software checks face position automatically when you submit online. Meeting these Schengen visa photo specifications for face framing is essential:

  • Position: Face directly forward, centered both horizontally and vertically
  • Expression: Neutral — mouth closed, no smile, no raised eyebrows
  • Eyes: Open, clearly visible, not obscured by hair, lashes, or glare
  • Head tilt: Not permitted — must be perfectly straight
  • Gaze: Looking directly at the camera lens
  • Lighting: Even frontal illumination, no shadows across nose or chin, no red-eye

A good Schengen visa photo checker will automatically scan your image for these biometric parameters — flagging head tilt, closed eyes, or shadows before you submit, saving you from a costly rejection.

Dress Code and What Not to Wear

There is no formal dress code for a Schengen visa photo, but several items are specifically prohibited and others are strongly advised against.

Wear:

  • Regular, everyday clothing in medium or dark tones
  • Solid colors that contrast clearly against the white background

Do not wear:

  • White or near-white tops (they blend into the background)
  • Hats, caps, or beanies of any kind
  • Sunglasses or prescription glasses — glasses are not permitted in Schengen photos as of 2015, per ICAO biometric standards
  • Military, police, or other uniforms (except for official duty applications)
  • Heavy accessories or large jewelry that distract from the face

Head coverings are allowed only for verified religious or medical reasons. If worn, the entire face must be fully visible — from hairline to chin, ear to ear — with no shadows cast on the face.

Schengen Visa Photo Sample: What Accepted vs. Rejected Looks Like

The most common reasons a Schengen visa photo gets rejected:

  • Wrong dimensions — not 35×45mm or incorrect face ratio
  • Colored, textured, or shadowed background
  • Glasses (any type — prescription, reading, or fashion)
  • Photo older than 6 months
  • Smiling or open-mouth expression
  • Blurry, low-resolution, or over-edited image
  • Incorrect file format or oversized digital file

DIY Schengen Visa Photo: Take It at Home Step-by-Step

A Schengen visa photo DIY approach works perfectly well if you follow the process carefully. You don't need a professional Schengen visa photo studio near me search or an expensive high street appointment — a decent smartphone and the right online tool are all you need.

  1. Stand or sit against a plain white wall (or hang a white sheet as a backdrop)
  2. Position yourself at least 1 metre from the wall to avoid shadow
  3. Use soft natural light from a window directly in front of you, or a ring light
  4. Set your phone camera to eye level — not above or below your face
  5. Remove glasses, hats, and heavy jewelry before shooting
  6. Keep a neutral expression, mouth closed, eyes open and looking at the lens
  7. Take 5–10 shots and select the sharpest, most evenly lit one
  8. Upload to a Schengen visa photo online tool to crop, resize, and verify compliance

The last step is the most important — manual cropping to the exact 35×45mm spec with correct face positioning is difficult to do accurately in a standard photo editor. An AI-powered Schengen visa photo cropper handles this automatically and also runs a built-in compliance check before you download.

Free Schengen Visa Photo Maker Tool: The Fastest Way in 2026

The easiest, most reliable way to get a fully compliant Schengen visa photo online free is to use an AI-powered photo maker. These tools eliminate every manual step that typically causes errors.

A good Schengen visa photo maker free tool — like PixPassport — does all of the following automatically:

  • Background removal and replacement — detects and replaces any background with compliant white, even if you photographed yourself against a grey wall or in a room
  • Smart face detection and cropping — positions your face at the exact correct ratio within the 35×45mm frame
  • Built-in Schengen visa photo checker — scans for head tilt, eye visibility, expression, shadow, and lighting issues before you download
  • Correct file output — exports at the right pixel dimensions, DPI, and file size for both digital submission and print
  • Printable photo sheet — generates a sheet of 4 or 6 photos ready for physical embassy or consulate submission

The entire process — from selfie upload to downloadable compliant photo — takes under two minutes. No Schengen visa photo studio near me search required, no appointment, no travel, and no cost.

👉 Try the free Schengen visa photo maker now: Create Your Schengen Photo at PixPassport

Schengen Visa Photo for Babies and Children

Every applicant — including newborns — needs their own individual Schengen visa photo. The same 35×45mm size applies to children of all ages.

  • No other person should appear in the frame (no parental hands or faces)
  • Baby's eyes should be open if possible; closed eyes may be accepted at consulate discretion for very young infants
  • White background is mandatory
  • Full face must be clearly visible

Practical tip: Lay the baby on a white blanket and photograph from directly above. This naturally creates a white background, keeps the face centered, and avoids the shadow problem you get when trying to hold an infant upright. Upload to a Schengen visa photo editor free tool to crop and verify compliance.

Schengen Visa Application: What Else You'll Need

Beyond your photo, a standard Schengen visa application requires:

  • Valid passport — valid for at least 3 months beyond your intended stay, with at least 2 blank pages
  • Completed application form — varies by consulate; always download from the official embassy website
  • Travel insurance — minimum €30,000 medical coverage, valid across the entire Schengen Area
  • Proof of accommodation — hotel bookings, host invitation, or rental confirmation for the full stay
  • Proof of sufficient funds — recent bank statements (usually 3–6 months)
  • Round-trip flight itinerary — confirmed or provisional booking showing entry and exit
  • Purpose of travel documentation — varies by category (tourism, business, family visit, study, etc.)

Fees and Processing Times

The standard Schengen visa fee in 2026 is €90 for adults and €45 for children aged 6–12. Children under 6 are exempt. Fees are non-refundable regardless of outcome.

Standard processing takes 15 calendar days, though this can extend to 30–45 days during peak travel periods (June–August, Christmas). Apply at least 6–8 weeks before your planned travel date. Many consulates require an in-person biometric appointment for fingerprinting — book this as early as possible, as slots fill up fast.

Top Tips for Photo and Application Approval

  1. Use a free Schengen visa photo tool — don't try to manually crop and resize. AI tools are faster and more accurate.
  2. Run a compliance check before submitting — a Schengen visa photo checker catches errors you may miss visually.
  3. Apply to the right consulate — submit to the consulate of your primary destination or first point of entry.
  4. Don't wait on insurance — travel insurance is required at application time, not just travel time.
  5. Be consistent across documents — travel dates, accommodation addresses, and financial information must align across every document submitted.
  6. Keep digital copies of everything — save PDFs of all submitted documents in case of follow-up requests.

Conclusion: Nail Your Schengen Visa Photo and Travel with Confidence

The Schengen visa photo requirements are precise — 35×45mm (3.5×4.5cm), white background, face filling 70–80% of the frame, no glasses, no shadow, neutral expression, taken within 6 months. Nail every one of these specifications and you remove one of the most common causes of visa rejection entirely.

Instead of searching for a Schengen visa photo near me or visiting a studio, use a free Schengen visa photo online tool to create a perfectly compliant image at home in minutes. It's faster, cheaper, and just as accepted by every consulate worldwide.

👉 Get your compliant Schengen visa photo in 2 minutes — free: PixPassport – Free Schengen Visa Photo Maker

title: Australia Visa Photo Requirements 2026 – Size, Specs, Background & Free Online Maker Tool
description: Complete Australia visa photo guide 2026. Official size (35x45mm), background rules, face specs, digital editing rules, and use our free online Australia visa photo maker tool to get a compliant photo in minutes. Covers passport photos too.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
Australia Visa Photo Requirements 2026: The Complete Official Guide

Your Australia visa photo is one of the first things an immigration officer checks — and it's one of the most common reasons applications are delayed or rejected outright. Australia's Department of Home Affairs enforces strict biometric photo standards that are checked both manually and by automated facial recognition software. Even a millimetre out on face height, a faint shadow, or a slightly off-white background can trigger an automatic rejection before your application is even opened.

This guide covers every single Australia visa photo requirement for 2026: the correct dimensions in mm, cm, and pixels; background color rules; face position and expression; what digital editing is and isn't allowed; the difference between visa and passport photo requirements; special rules for babies, children, and religious head coverings; and how to use a free Australia visa photo maker tool to generate a perfectly compliant image from your phone in under two minutes — no studio visit required.

Who Needs an Australian Visa Photo?

Australia requires visa photos from applicants of most nationalities, though the exact list changes periodically. Citizens of countries such as India, China, Nigeria, Pakistan, Indonesia, Bangladesh, and most of Southeast Asia, the Middle East, and Africa are typically required to apply for a visa before visiting Australia. Even nationalities that enjoy visa-free or eVisitor arrangements — such as the United States, United Kingdom, Canada, and most of Europe — may require a photo for certain long-stay, work, or student visa categories.

If you are applying for any of the following, a compliant photo is required: Tourist Visa (subclass 600), Student Visa (subclass 500), Work and Holiday Visa (subclass 462), Working Holiday Visa (subclass 417), Partner Visa, Skilled Migration Visa, or Australian Citizenship. The same core photo specifications apply across all visa and citizenship categories under the Department of Home Affairs.

Australia Visa Photo Size 2026: The Official Specifications

The Australia visa photo size follows ICAO (International Civil Aviation Organization) biometric standards, which are enforced uniformly across all Australian visa and passport categories. Here are the complete official dimensions:

  • Width: 35 mm to 40 mm
  • Height: 45 mm to 50 mm
  • Australia visa photo size in cm: 3.5 cm × 4.5 cm (minimum)
  • Australia visa photo size in inches: approximately 1.38" × 1.77"
  • Face height (chin to crown): 32 mm to 36 mm
  • Face coverage: 75–80% of the total photo frame
  • Digital resolution: Minimum 300 DPI; online ImmiAccount portal prefers 1200 × 1600 pixels
  • Digital file format: JPEG or JPG only
  • Maximum digital file size: 5000 KB (5 MB) for online upload
  • Recency: Taken within the last 6 months

👉 Get your compliant Australia visa photo in 2 minutes — completely free

Australia Visa Photo Australia Passport Photo

Powered by PixPassport

For printed submissions (paper visa applications or passport applications), photos must be printed on professional-grade photo paper using dye-sublimation or equivalent technique — never from a standard inkjet printer at home. Two identical prints are required for most paper-based applications.

Australia Visa Photo Size vs Australia Passport Photo Size: What's the Difference?

This is one of the most frequently asked questions — and the answer surprises many applicants. Australia visa photos and Australia passport photos share the same physical dimensions: 35–40 mm wide by 45–50 mm tall, with face height of 32–36 mm. However, there are a few important differences in how they are submitted and verified:

  • Visa photos (for Australian visas via ImmiAccount): Digital JPEG submission; no printed copy required for online applications. Background should be plain white or light grey.
  • Passport photos (for Australian passports via DFAT): Must be two printed copies on glossy or semi-gloss photo paper. Cannot be printed from an inkjet printer. The Australian Passport Office specifically recommends professional providers such as Australia Post.
  • Citizenship photos: Follow Department of Home Affairs standards identical to the visa photo spec; the background must be neutral or light grey and the photo must be unedited (background removal is not permitted for citizenship applications).

The critical takeaway: the same photo — if it meets all specifications — can be used for both visa and passport applications, provided it is recent, printed correctly for passport submission, and meets background requirements. Always confirm with your specific application portal before reusing a photo across document types.

Background Requirements for Australia Visa Photos

Background is the single most frequently failed element in Australian visa and passport photo submissions. The rules are precise and enforced by automated systems:

  • Visa applications: Plain white or very light grey background. The background must clearly contrast with your face and hair.
  • Passport applications: Plain neutral or light grey background. Pure white is also accepted.
  • Citizenship applications: Neutral or light grey only. The background must not be artificially removed or replaced — the original background from when the photo was taken must be present. This is a strict rule that makes AI background removal tools non-compliant for citizenship photos.
  • No shadows on the background — stand at least 1 metre from any wall when shooting
  • No patterns, textures, furniture, curtains, or other objects visible behind you
  • Evenly lit — no dark corners, no bright hotspots from flash or overhead lighting
  • No blue, cream, beige, yellow, or any tinted background — these are automatic rejections

If you're shooting a DIY Australia visa photo at home, stand in front of a plain white or very light grey wall with a window directly in front of you for soft, even natural light. A ring light or two lamps placed symmetrically on either side of your face also works well. Avoid camera flash — it creates harsh shadows and can cause red-eye.

Face Position, Expression, and Eye Requirements

Australian immigration uses ICAO-compliant biometric facial recognition software to process all digital photo submissions. This means your face positioning is machine-checked, not just human-reviewed. Every element below is measured algorithmically:

  • Face position: Centered horizontally and vertically in the frame — no off-center tilting
  • Head orientation: Facing directly forward, both ears fully visible and not obscured by hair
  • Expression: Neutral, natural resting face — mouth closed, no smile, no frown, no raised eyebrows
  • Eyes: Open, looking directly at the camera lens, not looking up, down, or sideways
  • Head tilt: Zero tilt permitted — head must be perfectly upright and straight
  • Facial features: All features must be fully visible — chin, both cheekbones, forehead, both eyes, nose, and mouth
  • Lighting on face: Uniform frontal illumination, no shadows on nose, chin, neck, or either side of the face
  • Red-eye: Not permitted — avoid flash photography to eliminate this
  • Focus: Sharp, clear image — no motion blur, no digital grain, no pixelation

Australia is notably stricter than many countries on the both-ears-visible requirement. Even partial hair covering of one ear can cause a rejection in automated biometric screening. Pull hair back or tuck it behind your ears before taking the photo.

Glasses Rules: Can You Wear Glasses in Your Australia Visa Photo?

As of 2026, the official position from the Department of Home Affairs is that glasses are strongly discouraged in Australian visa and passport photos, and for most applicants, removing glasses is the safest approach. Here are the precise rules:

  • If you can remove your glasses, you must do so for the photo.
  • If you cannot remove glasses for genuine medical reasons, the frames must not obscure your eyes, and there must be zero lens glare or reflection.
  • Tinted lenses (including prescription sunglasses) are not permitted in any case.
  • If you are submitting with glasses for medical reasons, you may need to provide a signed medical certificate from a registered practitioner that includes their full name, registration number, and practice address and phone number.
  • Vision impairment or discomfort alone is not an acceptable medical reason to wear glasses in a photo.

Hearing aids are permitted — they do not need to be removed for the photo.

Australia Visa Photo: Digital Editing Rules 2026

One of the most searched questions in 2026 is: "Is digital editing allowed for Australia visa photos?" The answer is nuanced and depends on the type of application:

  • For visa applications (ImmiAccount online portal): Cropping, resizing, and background standardisation using AI tools is generally accepted, provided the resulting image accurately represents your true appearance.
  • For citizenship applications: The Department of Home Affairs explicitly states photos must be unedited. Specifically: do not remove background, moles, wrinkles, scars, or red-eye. The original captured image must be submitted.
  • For passport applications (DFAT): The Australian Passport Office discourages AI-generated or heavily processed images and specifically warns of identity fraud risk with certain online services.
  • What is never acceptable regardless of visa type: Smoothing skin, altering facial features, changing eye color, adding makeup digitally, generating a synthetic face, or using beauty filters. These constitute fraud and are increasingly caught by biometric screening.

EXIF data is increasingly being reviewed by automated systems. When in doubt, use a reputable Australia visa photo online tool that produces technically compliant output without altering facial features.

Dress Code and Appearance Rules

Australian visa and passport photo guidelines include specific rules on what you should and shouldn't wear:

  • Clothing: Everyday clothing is fine. Avoid uniforms, military camouflage, or clothing that resembles an official uniform. Solid colours that contrast with the white/grey background are recommended.
  • Makeup: Minimal, natural-looking makeup is permitted. It should not significantly alter your natural appearance — heavy contouring or theatrical makeup is not acceptable.
  • Jewelry: Small earrings and a necklace are permitted as long as they don't create reflections, cast shadows, or cover any part of your face.
  • Glasses: Remove them — see the detailed glasses section above.
  • Head coverings (religious): Permitted if worn habitually for religious reasons. Must be plain (no patterns, not even small ones), must show your full face from bottom of chin to top of forehead, both edge of the face including outer eye corners must be visible, and the covering must cast no shadows on your face or background. Secular hats, caps, beanies, and fashion headwear are not permitted.
  • Head coverings (medical): Permitted if worn for a medical condition. Medical documentation may be required.

Special Requirements: Australia Visa Photo for Babies and Children

Every visa and passport applicant — including newborns — requires their own individual photo. Children cannot be included in a parent's photo, and no other person's hands, face, or body should appear in the frame at all. Here are the child-specific rules:

  • The same 35–40 mm × 45–50 mm dimensions apply to all ages
  • The child must be photographed alone — no parents, carers, toys, pacifiers, or prams visible
  • For babies and infants, eyes do not need to be fully open (the Australian Passport Office acknowledges this is impractical for newborns)
  • For infants under approximately 12 months, an open mouth is acceptable in the photo
  • White background is still required — use a plain white blanket or sheet as the backdrop
  • Full face must be clearly visible — no profile shots or tilted-head shots

Practical tip for baby photos: Lay the baby on a white blanket on a flat surface and photograph from directly above. This creates a natural white background, keeps the face in the center of the frame, and eliminates the shadow problem you get when trying to hold an infant upright. Upload to an Australia visa photo editor free tool to crop and check compliance before submitting.

👉 Get your compliant Australia visa photo in 2 minutes — completely free

Australia Visa Photo Australia Passport Photo

Powered by PixPassport

How to Take a DIY Australia Visa Photo at Home: Step-by-Step

You do not need to visit a photo studio to get a compliant Australia visa photo. A modern smartphone and a few minutes of setup are all it takes. Follow this step-by-step process:

  1. Find the right background: Stand against a plain white or very light grey wall, or hang a white bedsheet flat behind you. Make sure the wall is smooth and shadow-free.
  2. Control your lighting: Position yourself facing a large window so natural daylight illuminates your face evenly. If indoors without good natural light, place two lamps of equal brightness on either side of your face at eye level.
  3. Set your distance from the background: Stand or sit at least 1 metre away from the wall or sheet to prevent shadows falling behind you.
  4. Set up your camera: Use the rear camera (not the selfie camera) of your smartphone for better resolution and less distortion. Place the phone at eye level, ideally on a tripod or propped against something stable.
  5. Frame yourself correctly: Your head, neck, and upper shoulders should be visible in the frame. Center your face in the viewfinder.
  6. Prepare your appearance: Remove glasses, hats, and heavy jewelry. Pull hair back so both ears are fully visible. Ensure your full forehead is visible.
  7. Set your expression: Neutral face, mouth closed, eyes open and looking directly at the lens. Relax your face — no smiling, no squinting.
  8. Take multiple shots: Take 8–10 shots and select the sharpest, most evenly lit one where your expression and head position are exactly right.
  9. Upload to a free Australia visa photo tool: Use a free online Australia visa photo maker to crop to the exact 35×45 mm specification, verify face height, and check background compliance before downloading and submitting.

Free Australia Visa Photo Maker Tool: Create a Compliant Photo in 2 Minutes

The fastest and most accurate way to get a compliant Australia visa photo online is to use an AI-powered photo tool. Rather than manually cropping in a general-purpose editor (which makes precise face-height measurement nearly impossible), a dedicated tool handles everything automatically:

  • Crops your photo to the exact 35×45 mm Australian visa specification
  • Measures and adjusts face height to fall within the required 32–36 mm chin-to-crown range
  • Replaces non-compliant backgrounds with plain white using AI background removal
  • Checks for shadows, head tilt, closed eyes, and other automatic rejection triggers
  • Outputs a JPEG at the correct resolution and file size for ImmiAccount digital submission
  • Generates a print-ready sheet with multiple photos for paper-based applications

👉 Create your free compliant Australia visa photo now: PixPassport – Free Australia Visa Photo Maker Tool

Australia Passport Photo Requirements 2026

Australian passport photo requirements are set by the Department of Foreign Affairs and Trade (DFAT) and align with ICAO biometric standards. The specifications are nearly identical to the visa photo rules with a few key differences in submission format:

  • Size: 35–40 mm wide × 45–50 mm tall — same as visa photo
  • Face height: 32–36 mm from chin to crown — same as visa photo
  • Background: Plain neutral or light grey — slightly different from visa (which accepts white)
  • Submission: Two identical printed photos required on glossy or semi-gloss photo paper; standard inkjet printing is not acceptable
  • Source: The Australian Passport Office strongly recommends professional providers, particularly Australia Post or an equivalent overseas provider. DFAT explicitly advises caution with online passport photo services due to identity fraud risk.
  • Recency: Taken within the past 6 months
  • No glasses: Enforced — remove all eyewear unless medically exempted
  • Both ears visible: This requirement is strictly checked for Australian passports

Common reasons for Australian passport photo rejection include: incorrect printed dimensions, head size outside the 32–36 mm range, background color issues, shadows (even subtle ones under the chin are flagged), photos printed on standard copy paper, or any digital manipulation of the image.

Australia Visa Photo Checker: Run a Compliance Check Before You Submit

Before uploading your photo to ImmiAccount or attaching it to a paper application, always run it through a dedicated Australia visa photo checker. A good compliance checker will scan your image for:

  • Correct dimensions and face height within the 32–36 mm range
  • Background compliance — white/light grey, shadow-free
  • Face position — centered, forward-facing, no tilt
  • Eyes open and clearly visible
  • No head covering (unless flagged as religious exception)
  • Even lighting — no bright or dark patches on the face
  • No glasses glare
  • File size within the 5 MB limit and JPEG format

Running a pre-submission compliance check takes 30 seconds and can save you the cost, time, and frustration of a rejected application. An Australia visa photo online free tool from PixPassport includes a built-in compliance checker as part of the generation process.

How to Upload Your Australia Visa Photo to ImmiAccount

When applying for an Australian visa online via ImmiAccount (the Department of Home Affairs online portal), follow these steps to upload your digital photo correctly:

  1. Log in to your ImmiAccount at immi.homeaffairs.gov.au and navigate to your active application
  2. In the photo upload section, select "Upload Photo"
  3. Your file must be in JPEG or JPG format, maximum 5 MB
  4. The system will automatically scan the image with biometric software — if any specification is out of range, you'll receive an error message
  5. If your photo fails the automated check, you will need to retake and re-upload before proceeding
  6. Preferred resolution for online upload is 1200 × 1600 pixels (equivalent to approximately 300 DPI at 35×45 mm)

Do not try to stretch or artificially inflate a low-resolution photo to meet pixel requirements. The biometric software will flag low-resolution or interpolated images. Always start with a high-resolution original shot from a modern smartphone camera.

Australia Visa Photo vs. Schengen, US, and UK Visa Photo: Key Differences

If you're a frequent international traveller applying for multiple visas, here's how Australian visa photo specifications compare to other major destinations:

  • US visa photo: 2×2 inches (51×51 mm) square; Australia is rectangular (35×45 mm)
  • Schengen visa photo: Same 35×45 mm as Australia; background is pure white; face coverage is 70–80%
  • UK visa photo: 35×45 mm with a light grey or cream background; slightly different face coverage ratio
  • Canada visa photo: 35×45 mm with a white or off-white background — very similar to Australia

A photo taken for an Australian visa application will often be compliant for Schengen and Canadian visa applications with only minor background adjustments. A US visa photo, being square, will always require a separate crop.

Top 10 Reasons Australian Visa Photos Get Rejected in 2026

  1. Incorrect dimensions — face height outside the 32–36 mm range is the most common automated rejection trigger
  2. Wrong background colour — cream, beige, light blue, or patterned backgrounds are rejected
  3. Shadows — shadows on the background or across the face, even subtle ones, trigger automatic flags
  4. Glasses glare or reflection — even faint lens reflection fails biometric checks
  5. Head not straight — even a few degrees of tilt is picked up by biometric software
  6. Hair covering ears — both ears must be fully visible in Australian submissions
  7. Low resolution — blurry, pixelated, or re-saved-multiple-times JPEG photos
  8. Smile or non-neutral expression — any smile, frown, or raised eyebrow can flag
  9. Photo older than 6 months — particularly flagged if your appearance has changed (new haircut, weight change, ageing)
  10. Wrong file format or size — anything other than JPEG under 5 MB is immediately rejected by ImmiAccount

Australia Visa Photo Near Me: Studio vs. DIY vs. Online Tool

If you've been searching for Australia visa photo near me, here's an honest comparison of your three main options in 2026:

  • Photo studio or chemist (e.g. Australia Post, Chemist Warehouse, Harvey Norman): Reliable, professionally compliant, and recommended by DFAT for passport photos specifically. Cost: typically AUD $15–$25 for two prints. The main downside is time — you need to travel, queue, and wait. Not always convenient if applying from outside Australia.
  • DIY smartphone photo: Free, but requires careful setup. Easy to get wrong (shadows, face height errors, wrong crop). Best done with a tripod and good natural lighting. Needs to be processed by a compliant cropping tool afterward.
  • Free online Australia visa photo maker tool: The fastest and most convenient option for online visa applications. Tools like PixPassport handle all the technical specifications — cropping, face height, background removal, resolution — automatically. Takes 2 minutes from upload to download. Free, no login required. For passport applications, you can also generate a print-ready file and print at a local chemist or photo lab.

Quick Reference: Australia Visa Photo Requirements Summary 2026

  • Size: 35–40 mm × 45–50 mm (portrait, not square)
  • Size in cm: 3.5 cm × 4.5 cm
  • Size in inches: ~1.38" × 1.77"
  • Face height: 32–36 mm chin to crown
  • Face coverage: 75–80% of frame
  • Background: Plain white or very light grey (no shadows, no patterns)
  • Expression: Neutral, mouth closed, no smile
  • Eyes: Open, looking at lens
  • Glasses: Remove — not permitted
  • Head tilt: Zero — perfectly upright
  • Both ears: Must be fully visible
  • Recency: Within last 6 months
  • Digital format: JPEG, max 5 MB
  • Digital resolution: Min 300 DPI (prefer 1200×1600 px)
  • Print: Two identical prints on glossy/semi-gloss photo paper (for paper applications)

Frequently Asked Questions: Australia Visa Photo 2026

Can I take my own Australia visa photo at home?
Yes. You can take your photo at home with a smartphone and upload it to a free Australia visa photo online tool for cropping and compliance verification. For paper passport applications, DFAT recommends professional providers, but for online visa applications via ImmiAccount, a DIY photo processed through a compliant online tool is fully accepted.

Is my Australia visa photo the same size as a passport photo?
Yes — both share the same 35–40 mm × 45–50 mm dimensions and 32–36 mm face height requirement. The differences are in background colour specification and submission format (digital vs. printed).

Can I use a photo from 2024 for my 2026 visa application?
Only if it was taken within the last 6 months. A 2024 photo is outside the 6-month validity window and must be retaken.

Is digital editing allowed for Australia visa photos?
Cropping and resizing is always permitted. AI background replacement is generally accepted for visa applications. For citizenship applications, no editing of any kind is permitted — the original unmodified photo must be submitted.

Do babies need a separate Australia visa photo?
Yes. Every applicant, including newborns, requires their own individual photo. Baby photos follow the same size specifications but with relaxed expression and eye-open requirements for very young infants.

Can I wear a hijab or headscarf in my Australia visa photo?
Yes, if worn habitually for religious reasons. The covering must be plain (no patterns), must not cast shadows on your face, and your full face from chin to forehead must remain clearly visible with both side edges of your face also visible.

How do I check if my Australia visa photo meets requirements before submitting?
Use a dedicated Australia visa photo checker tool that scans for all biometric parameters — face height, background, lighting, expression, and file format — before you upload to ImmiAccount.

Conclusion: Get Your Australia Visa Photo Right the First Time in 2026

The Australia visa photo requirements for 2026 are precise but manageable: 35–40 mm wide by 45–50 mm tall, face height 32–36 mm, plain white or light grey background, no glasses, both ears visible, neutral expression, eyes open, taken within the last 6 months, submitted as a JPEG under 5 MB. Miss any one of these and your application faces delays or outright rejection — often before a human officer even reviews it.

The easiest, fastest, and most cost-effective solution in 2026 is to take a clear smartphone photo at home and process it through a free Australia visa photo maker tool. You get a biometrically accurate, compliant image in minutes — without studio appointments, without queuing, and without cost.

title: Country-Wise Visa Photo Requirements Comparison 2026 — Australia vs USA vs UK vs Canada vs Schengen
description: The definitive 2026 comparison of visa photo requirements across Australia, USA, UK, Canada, and all Schengen countries. Official photo sizes in mm and pixels, background color rules, head coverage percentages, glasses policy, and file format specs — all sourced from official government authorities including Department of Home Affairs, US State Department, UKVI, IRCC, and EU Visa Code.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
Country-Wise Visa Photo Requirements Comparison 2026 — The Complete Official Guide

One of the most common — and most avoidable — reasons visa applications get rejected worldwide is a non-compliant photograph. The problem is that every country has its own official photo standard, set by its own government authority, and the specifications differ significantly from one another. A photo that is perfectly compliant for a US visa application will be automatically rejected at a UK consulate. A photo that passes Australia's ImmiAccount portal will fail on Canada's IRCC visa portal because the dimensions don't match.

This guide compares the official visa photo requirements for 2026 across the five most commonly applied-for travel destinations: Australia (Department of Home Affairs), United States (US Department of State), United Kingdom (UKVI — UK Visas and Immigration), Canada (IRCC — Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada), and the Schengen Area (EU Visa Code, Regulation EC 810/2009). Every specification in this guide is sourced from official government portals and verified against current published requirements.

Quick Comparison: Visa Photo Size by Country 2026

Country / Authority Size (mm) Size (inches) Aspect Ratio Digital Pixels
🇦🇺 Australia (DHA) 35–40 × 45–50 mm 1.38" × 1.77" Portrait (rectangular) Min 300 DPI; prefer 1200×1600 px
🇺🇸 United States (State Dept.) 51 × 51 mm (2×2 in) 2" × 2" Square (1:1) 600–1200 × 600–1200 px; max 240KB JPEG
🇬🇧 United Kingdom (UKVI) 35 × 45 mm 1.38" × 1.77" Portrait (rectangular) JPEG or PNG; 50KB–10MB
🇨🇦 Canada — Visa (IRCC) 35 × 45 mm 1.38" × 1.77" Portrait (rectangular) Digital upload; JPEG; IRCC portal
🇨🇦 Canada — Passport (IRCC) 50 × 70 mm 1.97" × 2.76" Portrait (rectangular) 420×540 px (online) — 2 printed required
🇪🇺 Schengen Area (EU Visa Code) 35 × 45 mm 1.38" × 1.77" Portrait (rectangular) 413×531 px at 300 DPI; max 240KB JPEG

Key takeaway: The US is the only major destination requiring a square photo. Canada uses two different sizes — a smaller 35×45mm for visa applications and a uniquely large 50×70mm for passports. All other destinations use the standard 35×45mm portrait format.

Background Color Requirements by Country — 2026

Background color is the single most frequently failed element in international photo submissions. Each country has a specific official requirement, and they are not the same:

Country Official Background White Accepted? Authority
🇦🇺 Australia Plain white or very light grey ✅ Yes Department of Home Affairs
🇺🇸 United States Plain white or off-white only ✅ Yes (required) US Department of State / travel.state.gov
🇬🇧 United Kingdom Cream or light grey — NOT white ❌ No — white rejected HM Passport Office / UKVI
🇨🇦 Canada Visa Plain white or light-coloured ✅ Yes IRCC / canada.ca
🇨🇦 Canada Passport Plain white (no grey, no off-white) ✅ Yes (mandatory) IRCC / canada.ca
🇪🇺 Schengen Light grey or neutral light background ⚠️ Varies by consulate EU Visa Code / ICAO; consulate-level spec

⚠️ Critical Warning: UK Background Rule

The UK is one of the most commonly misunderstood countries for background color. HM Passport Office and UKVI specifically require a cream or light grey background — not white. Pure white backgrounds can confuse the automated verification system and are a documented rejection cause. This is the opposite of the US requirement, where white is mandatory. If you are applying to both destinations, you need two separate photos with different background colors.

Head Size and Face Coverage Requirements by Country

Country Head Height (mm) Face Coverage (%) Standard
🇦🇺 Australia 32–36 mm (chin to crown) 75–80% of frame ICAO / Department of Home Affairs
🇺🇸 United States 25–35 mm (1"–1⅜") 50–69% of frame US Dept. of State / 9 FAM 403.9-4
🇬🇧 United Kingdom 29–34 mm (chin to crown) 65–75% of frame HM Passport Office / UKVI
🇨🇦 Canada Visa 31–36 mm (chin to crown) ~70–80% of frame IRCC / canada.ca
🇨🇦 Canada Passport 31–36 mm (chin to crown) ~44–51% of 70mm frame IRCC / canada.ca
🇪🇺 Schengen 32–36 mm (chin to crown) 70–80% of frame EU Visa Code / ICAO Doc 9303

🚨 Critical Difference: US vs Australia/Schengen Head Size

The US requires the face to cover only 50–69% of the photo height. Australia and Schengen require 70–80%. This is the biggest practical difference between destinations. A photo cropped correctly for a US visa will have a face that is too small for an Australian or Schengen visa submission — and vice versa. You cannot use the same photo file for both. Separate crops are always required.

Glasses Policy by Country — 2026

Country Glasses Permitted? Medical Exception? Policy Since
🇦🇺 Australia ❌ Not permitted No Enforced by DHA biometric standard
🇺🇸 United States ❌ Not permitted Yes — signed medical statement required November 1, 2016
🇬🇧 United Kingdom ❌ Not permitted Yes — rare medical cases only 2016 (passport); enforced for visas
🇨🇦 Canada Visa ✅ Allowed (conditions) N/A — permitted with clear lenses, no glare IRCC current guidance
🇨🇦 Canada Passport ❌ Not permitted Not specified Since 2017
🇪🇺 Schengen ❌ Not recommended / often rejected Consulate-dependent Updated 2022 Schengen biometric guidelines

✅ Canada Visa Exception — Unique Glasses Rule

Canada's IRCC is the only major immigration authority that still permits prescription glasses in visa photos under its current published guidance — provided the lenses are completely clear and non-tinted, the frames do not cover any part of the eyes, and there is absolutely no glare or reflection. This rule does not extend to Canadian passport photos, where glasses have been banned since 2017. For every other destination in this comparison, the universal advice for 2026 is: remove all eyewear before your photo is taken.

Digital File Format and Upload Requirements by Country

Country File Format Max File Size Min Resolution Upload Portal
🇦🇺 Australia JPEG / JPG 5 MB 300 DPI (prefer 1200×1600 px) ImmiAccount — immi.homeaffairs.gov.au
🇺🇸 United States JPEG only (no HEIC, PNG, PDF) 240 KB 600×600 px min; 1200×1200 px max CEAC — ceac.state.gov (DS-160)
🇬🇧 United Kingdom JPEG or PNG 10 MB 50 KB minimum GOV.UK visa application portal
🇨🇦 Canada Visa JPEG 4 MB 35×45mm proportional IRCC Online Application — canada.ca
🇨🇦 Canada Passport JPEG (online); printed required 4 MB (online renewals) 420×540 px Two printed copies — photographer stamp required
🇪🇺 Schengen JPEG 240 KB 413×531 px at 300 DPI VFS Global / TLS Contact / Consulate portal

🔵 US File Size Warning — 240KB is Strict

The US CEAC portal enforces a 240KB maximum file size — the smallest hard limit of any major destination. Raw smartphone photos are typically 3–8MB and must be compressed before upload. The State Department also specifies a maximum JPEG compression ratio of 20:1 — aggressive compression that creates visible blocking artifacts may be rejected during manual consular review. The UK's 10MB limit is 40 times more generous, making digital submission far more forgiving.

Printed Photo Requirements — How Many Copies?

Country Printed Copies Paper Type Required Special Requirements
🇦🇺 Australia (Visa) Digital only (online applications) N/A for online JPEG upload to ImmiAccount
🇦🇺 Australia (Passport) 2 identical prints Glossy or semi-gloss photo paper No inkjet home printing; DFAT recommends Australia Post
🇺🇸 United States (DS-160) Digital only (DS-160 online) N/A for online DS-260 (immigrant visa): 2 prints at interview
🇬🇧 United Kingdom 2 prints for paper applications Matte or glossy photo paper Optional 16-char photo code from approved UK retailers
🇨🇦 Canada (Visa) Digital upload (online applications) N/A for online No photographer stamp required for visa photos
🇨🇦 Canada (Passport) 2 identical prints Matte finish on high-quality paper Photographer's name, address, and date must be on back
🇪🇺 Schengen 2 identical prints High-quality photo paper, min 160 GSM; 400–600 DPI print resolution One for application form, one for visa sticker

🇨🇦 Canada Passport — Photographer Stamp is Mandatory

Canada's IRCC requires that for printed passport photo submissions, the photographer's name, address, and the date the photo was taken must be written or stamped on the back of one of the two photos. This is a uniquely Canadian requirement not found at any other major destination. DIY printed photos without a photographer's endorsement are technically non-compliant for passport applications (though acceptable for digital visa submissions).

Recency — How Old Can Your Visa Photo Be?

Country Maximum Age of Photo Notes
🇦🇺 AustraliaWithin last 6 monthsDepartment of Home Affairs requirement
🇺🇸 United StatesWithin last 6 monthsMust reflect current appearance; State Dept. rule
🇬🇧 United KingdomWithin last 1 monthHM Passport Office requires very recent photos
🇨🇦 CanadaWithin last 6 monthsIRCC requirement — must reflect current appearance
🇪🇺 SchengenWithin last 6 monthsSome Schengen consulates (Denmark, Iceland, Norway) have historically requested 3 months — always verify with specific consulate

🇬🇧 UK Recency Requirement is the Strictest

The UK's HM Passport Office requires photos taken within the last month — far stricter than the 6-month window allowed by the US, Australia, Canada, and most Schengen countries. If you are applying to the UK and another destination simultaneously, ensure your photos are taken within a few days of each other to avoid a situation where your UK photo is still valid but your other photos have expired, or vice versa.

Complete Side-by-Side Comparison: All Key Specifications

Specification 🇦🇺 Australia 🇺🇸 USA 🇬🇧 UK 🇨🇦 Canada Visa 🇪🇺 Schengen
Photo size35–40×45–50mm51×51mm (square)35×45mm35×45mm35×45mm
BackgroundWhite or light greyWhite / off-white onlyCream or light grey (NOT white)White or light-colouredLight grey / neutral
Head height32–36mm25–35mm (1–1⅜")29–34mm31–36mm32–36mm
Face coverage75–80%50–69%65–75%~70–80%70–80%
Glasses❌ Not permitted❌ Not permitted (since 2016)❌ Not permitted✅ Allowed (clear, no glare)❌ Not recommended
File formatJPEGJPEG only (no HEIC)JPEG or PNGJPEGJPEG
Max file size5 MB240 KB (strict)10 MB4 MB240 KB
Recency6 months6 months1 month6 months6 months
Prints requiredDigital (visa); 2 prints (passport)Digital (DS-160); 2 prints (DS-260)2 prints or photo codeDigital upload only2 identical prints
Official authorityDept. of Home AffairsUS Dept. of StateUKVI / HM Passport OfficeIRCCEU Visa Code EC 810/2009

Can the Same Photo Be Used for Multiple Countries?

This is the most practical question for international travelers applying to multiple destinations simultaneously. The honest answer: rarely, and only under specific conditions. Here is what the official specifications tell us:

  • Australia + Schengen: Both use 35×45mm, 70–80% face coverage, and accept white/light grey backgrounds. A photo meeting Australian specs will generally also meet Schengen specs, provided the background is acceptable (light grey works for both). May be reusable if taken within 6 months.
  • Australia + Canada Visa: Both use 35×45mm and similar face coverage. However, Canada allows glasses and Australia does not. Background rules are compatible. Generally reusable if you don't wear glasses.
  • Australia/Schengen + UK: Same 35×45mm size. However, the UK requires a cream or light grey background while Australia accepts white. Face coverage also differs slightly (UK: 65–75% vs Australia: 75–80%). Not reliably reusable — different background and face proportion requirements.
  • Any destination + USA: The US requires a square 51×51mm photo with only 50–69% face coverage. No rectangular photo from any other destination can be used for the US. Always a separate photo is required.
  • Canada Passport + Canada Visa: Despite being the same country, Canadian passport photos (50×70mm) and visa photos (35×45mm) have different sizes. Cannot be reused for each other.

Common Mistakes When Applying to Multiple Countries

  1. Using a UK photo for a US application — wrong size (UK is 35×45mm, US is 51×51mm square)
  2. Using a US photo for Australia or Schengen — wrong aspect ratio and too little face coverage
  3. Using the same white-background photo for a UK visa — UK requires cream or light grey, not white
  4. Reusing a Canadian passport photo for a Canadian visa — different sizes (50×70mm vs 35×45mm)
  5. Submitting a 6-month-old photo to the UK — UK requires within 1 month, not 6
  6. Uploading a 5MB file to the US CEAC portal — US maximum is 240KB; file will be rejected automatically
  7. Wearing glasses in an Australian or US photo — not permitted in either; only Canadian visa allows it

2026 Visa Photo Requirements by Country — Final Summary

Official Governing Authorities

🇦🇺 Australia
Department of Home Affairs — immi.homeaffairs.gov.au
🇺🇸 United States
US Department of State — travel.state.gov / ceac.state.gov
🇬🇧 United Kingdom
UK Visas & Immigration (UKVI) — gov.uk/apply-uk-visa
🇨🇦 Canada
Immigration, Refugees and Citizenship Canada (IRCC) — canada.ca
🇪🇺 Schengen
EU Visa Code — Regulation (EC) No 810/2009 / ICAO Doc 9303
Biometric Standard
ICAO Document 9303 — International Civil Aviation Organization

Get a compliant visa photo for any country — completely free

Australia Visa Photo Schengen Visa Photo US Visa Photo

title: New Zealand Visa Photo Requirements 2026 — Official Size, Format & Upload Rules
description: Complete 2026 guide to New Zealand visa and NZeTA photo requirements sourced directly from Immigration New Zealand (immigration.govt.nz). Covers photo size (35×45mm for paper, 3:4 digital), file format (JPEG), face coverage (70–80%), glasses rules, lighting, background, and the no-AI-editing rule. Avoid rejection — check every requirement before you upload.
author: PixPassport Team
content:
New Zealand Visa Photo Requirements 2026 — The Complete Official Guide

Whether you are applying for a New Zealand visitor visa, a work visa, a student visa, or a New Zealand Electronic Travel Authority (NZeTA), one of the most overlooked steps is getting your photo right. Immigration New Zealand (INZ) uses automated face-recognition systems to verify uploaded photos, and photos that do not meet their technical standards will cause your application to be delayed — or in some cases, declined entirely.

Every requirement on this page is sourced directly from the official Immigration New Zealand photo standards page. We have not added our own interpretation — only clear explanations of what the government requires.

⚠️ 2026 Update: AI-Edited Photos Are Explicitly Banned

Immigration New Zealand has added a specific warning to their official photo page: photos that are AI-enhanced or digitally altered do not meet their standards. If INZ detects that your photo has been edited using AI tools, your visa or NZeTA application will be delayed or could be declined. This applies to filters, face-slimming, skin smoothing, background replacement, and contrast adjustments.

Quick Reference: New Zealand Visa Photo Specifications at a Glance

Specification Online / Digital Application Paper-Based Application
Photo size3:4 portrait aspect ratio (no fixed mm dimension)35 mm wide × 45 mm high
File formatJPG or JPEG onlyPhysical print (paper form instructions apply)
File size512 KB minimum — 3.14 MB maximumUp to 10 MB if submitted by professional photographer
Face coverage70% to 80% of the image70% to 80% of the image
RecencyTaken within the last 6 monthsTaken within the last 6 months
GlassesAllowed (prescription, clear, no glare, no heavy frames)Allowed (same rules)
BackgroundNeutral and plain — no objects or other peopleNeutral and plain
ExpressionNeutral, mouth closed, looking straight at cameraNeutral, mouth closed
AI/digital editing❌ Strictly prohibited — grounds for application delay or decline❌ Strictly prohibited
SelfiesOnly allowed in the NZeTA mobile appNot applicable
Official sourceImmigration New Zealand — immigration.govt.nz

Photo Size: Digital vs Paper Applications

New Zealand's photo size requirement differs depending on whether you are applying online or using a paper application form — and this is one of the most common sources of confusion.

For online visa and NZeTA applications: Immigration New Zealand does not specify a fixed dimension in millimetres. Instead, they require the photo to be taken in portrait mode with a 3:4 aspect ratio. This means the height of the image must be 1.33 times its width. A standard 3:4 photo taken on a smartphone in portrait orientation meets this requirement, provided it also meets all the other specifications below. The file must be between 512 KB and 3.14 MB and in JPG or JPEG format.

For paper-based visa applications: The print must be 35 mm wide and 45 mm high — this is the standard international passport photo format also used by Australia, Canada, the UK, and Schengen countries. Additional instructions for paper submissions are found on the paper application forms themselves.

📱 NZeTA Selfie Exception

Selfies are only acceptable if you are applying for a New Zealand Electronic Travel Authority (NZeTA) through the official mobile app, and only when the app gives you no other option. If you do take a selfie, Immigration New Zealand advises stretching your arm out as far as possible, holding the phone at eyebrow height, and ensuring the light source is behind the phone rather than behind your face. For all other online visa applications, selfies are not permitted.

The No-AI-Editing Rule — What Exactly Is Prohibited?

Immigration New Zealand is explicit about this on their official photo standards page. The photo must be natural and unaltered. The following edits are all prohibited, even minor ones:

  • Changing the colour, brightness, contrast, or sharpness of the image
  • Cropping your head and shoulders to place them on a plain background (background replacement)
  • Changing your facial features — including the size, shape, or colour of eyes, nose, ears, mouth, cheekbones, or eyebrows
  • Skin smoothing or face slimming
  • Digitally removing objects from the photo, especially around your face — glasses, jewellery, headbands, hats, or toys
  • Applying filters or beautification features
  • Using any AI editing tool

INZ also specifically warns that if your phone automatically applies filters or alters photos when you take them, you must turn this feature off before taking your visa photo. Many modern smartphones apply skin smoothing or portrait-mode effects by default — these must be disabled.

Face Positioning and Coverage

Your face must cover between 70% and 80% of the image and must be centred in the frame. This is the same face coverage range required by Schengen countries and similar to Australia's 75–80% standard, but notably wider than the US requirement of only 50–69% coverage.

Specifically, Immigration New Zealand requires:

  • You are looking straight at the camera — not turned to the side or on an angle
  • Your expression is neutral and your mouth is closed
  • Your eyes are open
  • The image is in focus and not blurry

Hair, Head Coverings, and Ears

Your hair must not cover your face or ears. The photo must show your ears unless you wear a scarf for religious or medical reasons. Head coverings must be removed unless they are worn for religious or medical reasons. If you wear a religious head covering, it must not cover your mouth or the sides of your face — your full facial features from forehead to chin and ear to ear must be visible.

Glasses Rules

New Zealand is more permissive about glasses than Australia, the United States, or the UK. You can wear prescription glasses in your New Zealand visa photo, provided:

  • The lenses are clear — no tinted lenses and no sunglasses
  • The frames are not heavy and do not cover your face
  • There is no glare or reflection in the photo from the lenses

Immigration New Zealand notes that if you are having issues with glare or reflection, it is best to remove your glasses for the photo.

Country Glasses Policy
🇳🇿 New Zealand✅ Allowed — clear lenses, no glare, no heavy frames
🇨🇦 Canada Visa✅ Allowed — clear lenses, no glare
🇦🇺 Australia❌ Not permitted
🇺🇸 United States❌ Not permitted (since 2016)
🇬🇧 United Kingdom❌ Not permitted
🇪🇺 Schengen⚠️ Not recommended — most consulates reject them

Lighting and Background

Lighting: There must be no shadows on your face or on the background. Your face must have good contrast against the background. Immigration New Zealand recommends standing 0.5 metres away from the background, with the photographer positioned 1.5 metres in front of you.

Background: The background must be neutral and plain. It should show only you — no other people, objects, or distracting elements. INZ specifically warns that patterned clothing (such as t-shirts with images or busy patterns) and flowery or patterned backgrounds can create technical issues with their automated face-recognition system.

Notably, unlike Australia (which specifies white or light grey) or the UK (which specifies cream or light grey), Immigration New Zealand does not mandate a specific background colour — only that it is neutral and plain. In practice, a plain white or light grey background is the safest choice as it provides maximum contrast with most skin tones and avoids the patterning issue.

Common Mistakes That Get New Zealand Visa Photos Rejected

  1. Using a photo taken more than 6 months ago — INZ requires the photo to reflect your current appearance
  2. Uploading a HEIC or PNG file — only JPG/JPEG is accepted for online applications
  3. File size below 512 KB — too small is rejected, not just too large
  4. File size above 3.14 MB — exceeds the digital upload limit (note: professional photographer submissions have a higher 10 MB limit)
  5. Using an AI background replacement tool — even if the result looks natural, it is explicitly prohibited
  6. Face covering less than 70% of the image — a common outcome of taking photos at arm's length
  7. Submitting a selfie for a visa (not NZeTA) application — selfies are only allowed in the NZeTA mobile app
  8. Leaving phone beauty filters active — many phones apply smoothing by default; this must be turned off
  9. Shadows on face or background — poor lighting is one of the most common automated rejection triggers
  10. Head turned or at an angle — you must face directly forward, not at a slight angle

New Zealand Visa Photo vs NZeTA Photo — Is There a Difference?

No. The same photo standards apply to both standard visa applications and NZeTA applications. The only difference is the selfie exception: selfies are only permitted when applying for a NZeTA via the official NZeTA mobile app, and only when that is the submission method the app requires. For all other submission methods — including desktop web applications — the same non-selfie photo standards apply.

New Zealand vs Australia vs Schengen — Key Differences

Specification 🇳🇿 New Zealand 🇦🇺 Australia 🇪🇺 Schengen
Digital size3:4 portrait ratioJPEG, no fixed ratio specified35×45mm equivalent
Paper size35×45mm35–40×45–50mm35×45mm
Max file size (digital)3.14 MB5 MBVaries by consulate (~240 KB–1 MB)
Min file size512 KBNot specifiedNot specified
Face coverage70–80%75–80%70–80%
Glasses✅ Allowed (conditions)❌ Not permitted⚠️ Not recommended
Background colourNeutral and plain (no colour specified)White or light greyLight grey / neutral
Recency6 months6 months6 months
File formatJPG/JPEG onlyJPEGJPEG
AI editing❌ Explicitly prohibited❌ Not permitted❌ Not permitted

Using a Professional Photographer

Immigration New Zealand states on their official website that they are more likely to accept your photo if it is taken by a professional photographer. When using a professional, the file size limit is raised from 3.14 MB to 10 MB. Make sure the photographer is familiar with INZ's requirements before they take your photo — not all passport photographers are aware of the specific 3:4 ratio and minimum file size requirements that INZ enforces.

✅ Official PDF from Immigration New Zealand

Immigration New Zealand publishes a downloadable PDF guide for photographers titled "Taking acceptable visa photos." This can be shared with your photographer before the session. It is available in English and Simplified Chinese from the official INZ website.

Complete New Zealand Visa Photo Checklist — Before You Upload

  • ☐ Photo taken within the last 6 months
  • ☐ File is JPG or JPEG format (not PNG, HEIC, or TIFF)
  • ☐ File size is between 512 KB and 3.14 MB (or up to 10 MB if from a professional photographer)
  • ☐ Aspect ratio is 3:4 portrait (height is 1.33× the width)
  • ☐ Face covers 70%–80% of the image
  • ☐ Face is centred and looking directly at the camera
  • ☐ Expression is neutral — mouth closed, eyes open
  • ☐ Head is straight, not tilted or turned
  • ☐ Ears are visible (unless covered for religious or medical reasons)
  • ☐ No head coverings (unless for religious or medical reasons)
  • ☐ If wearing glasses: clear lenses, no heavy frames, no glare
  • ☐ Background is neutral and plain — no objects, patterns, or other people
  • ☐ No shadows on face or background
  • ☐ No filters, AI edits, skin smoothing, or brightness adjustments applied
  • ☐ Phone auto-beauty or portrait mode has been turned off
  • ☐ Photo is not a photo of a photo (no screen captures of existing photos)

Official Source

🇳🇿 New Zealand
Immigration New Zealand (INZ)
immigration.govt.nz — Ministry of Business, Innovation and Employment
All specifications on this page were verified against the official INZ photo standards page as of May 2026. Requirements can change — always confirm at immigration.govt.nz before submitting your application.
